1: Theme. The seventh month — the most sacred month of the Jewish calendar (Trumpets, Atonement, Tabernacles) — and the community gathers “as one man.” The unity of the assembly prefigures the church’s gathering: “they were all together in one place” (Acts 2:1) when the Spirit came at Pentecost — itself another sacred season. Christ is the one who makes “one new man” from divided humanity (Ephesians 2:15), gathering the scattered community into the “one body” (Ephesians 4:4).
2: Type. Jeshua and Zerubbabel build the altar of the God of Israel. The Davidic prince (Zerubbabel) and the high priest (Jeshua) act together to restore sacrifice — the kingly-priestly partnership that Zechariah will interpret as a type of the one who “shall be a priest on his throne” (Zechariah 6:13). Christ, who is both King of kings and eternal High Priest, is the one in whom this dual office is permanently united. The altar they build is the shadow; Christ himself is both the altar and the sacrifice (Hebrews 13:10-12).
3: Theme. “They set the altar in its place, for fear was on them because of the peoples of the lands.” The act of worship despite fear — establishing the altar before any walls of protection were built — models the principle that access to God through the altar is the community’s primary defense. Christ is the altar (Hebrews 13:10) and the mediator through whom the hostile world is overcome: “In the world you will have tribulation. But take heart; I have overcome the world” (John 16:33). The altar set up in the face of fear is the type of faith that worships despite hostile circumstances.
4: Shadow. The Feast of Tabernacles (sukkôt) — the first festival celebrated by the returned community — commemorated the wilderness wandering (Leviticus 23:42-43). The returning community, dwelling in the vulnerability of incomplete restoration, enacts this festival that celebrates YHWH’s provision in the desert. Christ is the fulfillment of Tabernacles: “the Word became flesh and tabernacled (eskēnōsen) among us” (John 1:14). The divine dwelling in human vulnerability that the festival commemorated became literal in the incarnation.
5: Shadow. The twice-daily ʿōlat hattāmîḏ (continual burnt offering) — morning and evening lambs — the heartbeat of temple ritual. Christ is the fulfillment and end of the tāmîḏ: “he has no need, like those high priests, to offer sacrifices daily… he did this once for all when he offered up himself” (Hebrews 7:27). The daily repetition of the tāmîḏ was a built-in confession of incompleteness; Christ’s once-for-all offering is the completion that the daily sacrifice could only announce.
6: Theme. “But the foundation of the temple of YHWH had not yet been laid.” Sacrifice begins before the building is complete — the altar precedes the temple. Christ’s sacrifice on the cross preceded the building of the church: “I will build my church” (Matthew 16:18) — future tense, spoken before the cross. The altar was established before the building so that the building would be grounded in sacrifice rather than sacrifice grounded in the building. The cross is the foundation on which the church is built (1 Corinthians 3:11).
7: Type. Tyrian and Sidonian craftsmen hired for cedar — the Solomonic pattern replicated. The nations’ skilled labor contributing to the temple connects the restoration community to its greatest precedent. Christ builds his church with materials gathered from all nations: the Great Commission sends disciples to all nations not only to preach but to incorporate them as living stones in the spiritual temple (1 Peter 2:5; Ephesians 2:19-22). The Gentile craftsmen of the temple type become the Gentile believers of the church antitype.
8: Type. Second year, second month — the Solomonic timing. The Levites “from twenty years old and upward” appointed to supervise. Christ appoints his own supervisors for the building of his community: apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors, and teachers “to equip the saints for the work of ministry, for building up the body of Christ” (Ephesians 4:11-12). The Levitical supervisors of the second temple point to the Spirit-gifted ministers who oversee the construction of the living temple.
9: Theme. The Levitical families standing together (yāʿamdû kĕʾeḥāḏ, “stood as one”) to supervise the builders. The unity of the leadership in the building project prefigures the apostolic unity that characterized the early church: “they devoted themselves to the apostles’ teaching and the fellowship” (Acts 2:42). Christ prays for this unity: “that they may all be one, just as you, Father, are in me, and I in you” (John 17:21). Unified leadership enables unified building.
10: Shadow. The foundation-laying celebrated with priests bearing trumpets and Levitical singers with cymbals — the music of the temple dedication ceremony. The laying of the foundation is the moment when the sacred precinct is established. Christ is the cornerstone that makes the foundation sacred: “Behold, I am laying in Zion a stone, a cornerstone chosen and precious” (1 Peter 2:6, citing Isaiah 28:16). When the cornerstone is laid, the trumpet sounds and the community sings — the inauguration of the true temple at Easter is the event that all foundation-laying ceremonies anticipated.
11: Direct. “For he is good, for his steadfast love endures forever toward Israel.” This refrain — sung at the dedication of Solomon’s temple (2 Chronicles 5:13; Ezra 7:3) and now at the second temple’s foundation — is the central liturgical affirmation of the covenant. Paul applies the same confession to Christ: “all the promises of God find their Yes in him” (2 Corinthians 1:20). “His steadfast love endures forever” becomes in the NT “who shall separate us from the love of Christ?” (Romans 8:35) — the love that endures through exile, crucifixion, and death.
12: Theme. The elders who had seen the first temple wept at its lesser replacement. The grief of those who remember former glory is honored in Scripture. Christ speaks to this grief: “You mourn now, but your mourning will turn to joy” (John 16:20). Haggai 2:9 promises the second temple will surpass the first in glory — a promise fulfilled when Christ himself enters it (Luke 2:22-38; John 2:13-22) and declares it is his Father’s house. The weeping of the elders at the lesser building is answered by the presence of the greater Solomon in the lesser temple.
13: Theme. The mixed sound — weeping and shouting together, indistinguishable — defines the eschatological tension of the “already but not yet.” The restoration is real (shout!) but incomplete (weep!). The early church lived in the same tension: already redeemed, not yet glorified; already children of God, not yet revealed as such (Romans 8:18-25). Christ himself wept at Lazarus’s tomb (John 11:35) even knowing he would raise him — joy and grief are not mutually exclusive in the inauguration of the kingdom. The indistinguishable sound at Ezra 3:13 is the sonic image of inaugurated eschatology.
